narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted an 83% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students with the strongest signals of bar passage success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score of 24, which is notably lower than the typical applicant's score of around 37, making it the most influential concern in your profile. Next in importance, your Year 1 school decile of 5 fell short of the average 5.7, signaling to the model somewhat weaker early academic performance compared to peers. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 4 was below the typical 5.5, reinforcing that trend and nudging the odds further toward a predicted struggle on the bar. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, while not the top driver, was also under the average 3.2 and contributed in the same direction, highlighting academics as a key area of focus for the model. On a brighter note, your choice to go full-time stood out positively—since over 92% of students are in that category, it slightly pushed the prediction toward bar passage success, unlike part-time paths that can complicate things. Your middle-income bracket also offered a small favorable nudge, as it aligns well with many successful applicants. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having academics and test scores that lag behind the pack, even as your enrollment plans help balance things out. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—you have strengths the model recognized—but these metrics were the strongest predictors of bar outcomes in our data. Moving forward, I encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost that score, strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses or targeted study, and reapply once those stand out more; many students in similar situations turn things around and succeed. We're rooting for you to build on your full-time commitment and family support to make your next application shine. Keep pushing—you've got this.
